name: Cooking with Heat
tagline: Applying controlled heat to food to improve safety, digestibility, and calorie extraction
problem:
Many foods are marginal without heat: proteins can harbour pathogens (bacteria, parasites); some plants contain heat-labile toxins (lectins, cyanogenic glycosides) or enzyme inhibitors; starches in tubers and grains are less accessible raw. Chewing and digestion are metabolically expensive — heat denatures proteins, gelatinises starches, and breaks down cell walls, changing what calories a human gut can absorb per hour.

Cooking also concentrates flavours and enables combining ingredients into stews that homogenise nutrients across a group — a social technology as much as a chemical one.

The engineering problem is not merely temperature peaks but controlled heat transfer without burning, efficient fuel use, and containers that survive repeated thermal cycling. That couples cooking tightly to ceramics and later metals.

In space habitats, cooking is limited by the absence of open fire and the need for total smoke/volatile capture. Heat must be delivered via electrical induction or resistance, and off-gassing from oils and caramelisation must be scrubbed by the life-support system to prevent cabin atmosphere toxicity.
overview:
Cooking methods map to heat transfer modes. Direct radiation from coals or flame sears surfaces — good for Maillard browning, easy to overcook interiors. Conduction through stones (hot-rock boiling) heats liquids in organic containers (hides, bark) that would burn in flame. Convection in earth ovens (Hangi/Umu) and steam pits circulates moist heat evenly. Immersion boiling in pottery is the gateway to grains and legumes at scale.

Temperature bands matter: pasteurisation concepts (time at temperature) reduce pathogen risk. For plant toxins, specific knowledge replaces thermometers: slow simmering (to destroy lectins in beans) versus multiple water-change boiling (to leach cyanide from cassava). Boiling point changes with altitude: at 5000m, water boils at ~83°C, requiring significantly longer cooking times or pressure vessels.

Fuel efficiency is a design variable: insulated 'hay boxes' reuse residual heat; lids reduce evaporative losses; nested pots save energy. Ash and soot management affects food taste and long-term respiratory health.

Nutritionally, cooking can reduce some heat-sensitive vitamins (C, B12) while increasing bioavailability of others (Lycopene, Beta-carotene); the net depends on diet composition. The civilisational net effect has been overwhelmingly enabling for human brain expansion and population density.

principles.name: Denaturation and gelatinisation
principles.explanation: Heat unfolds complex proteins (meat) and swells starch granules (tubers), increasing surface area for digestive enzymes to work.

principles.name: Maillard Reaction
principles.explanation: A chemical reaction between amino acids and reducing sugars that gives browned food its distinctive flavour. Occurs rapidly above 140°C — difficult to achieve in boiling water.

principles.name: Thermal death time for microbes
principles.explanation: Pathogen reduction is a function of temperature and duration. 74°C (165°F) is the general 'instant kill' field standard for most foodborne bacteria.

principles.name: Boiling point depression
principles.explanation: Atmospheric pressure drops with altitude, lowering the maximum temperature of boiling water. High-altitude cooking requires 2x-4x more time for the same chemical transformation.

principles.name: Passive heat retention
principles.explanation: Once a vessel is at 100°C, it can be removed from fire and placed in an insulated pit. This saves 70% of fuel for long-simmer tasks like beans or tough meat.

components.name: Heat source
components.madeFrom: Hardwood coals (steady), charcoal, or dung (smoky/low heat)
components.criticalNote: Prefer glowing coals; direct flame impingement deposits bitter soot and can crack pottery

components.name: Cooking vessel
components.position: On stones, in coals, or suspended by tripod
components.madeFrom: Pottery (cord-marked/thick-walled), later metal; birch-bark baskets for hot-rock boiling
components.criticalNote: Thermal shock — preheat pottery near the fire before placing on coals

components.name: Hot stones / Baking stones
components.function: Stores and transfers heat in pits or boiling baskets
components.position: In the fire, then moved to the cooking vessel
components.madeFrom: Granite, basalt, or other dense non-porous rocks
components.criticalNote: Avoid 'river rocks' containing trapped water — they will explode when heated in fire

components.name: Stirring and handling tools
components.function: Moves food for even heating; avoids local scorch
components.madeFrom: Wood (Maple, Oak), bone, or green-wood tongs
components.criticalNote: Avoid toxic wood species like Yew or Oleander for spoons

rawMaterials.name: Water
earthLocations: Potable sources as per Sanitation entry
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Boil first if source is suspect; discard leaching water for toxic plants

rawMaterials.name: Biomass fuel
earthLocations: Dry hardwoods provide the best 'cooking coal' beds
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Direct solar concentrators or electric resistance coils
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Split wood to 'wrist-thickness' for rapid coal production

rawMaterials.name: Bio-available starch and protein
earthLocations: Tubers (Potato, Cassava), Grains (Wheat, Maize), Game meat
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Wash, peel, and inspect for parasites (worms) before heating

buildSteps.title: Hearth preparation and coal bed
buildSteps.description: Establish a fire using hardwood. Allow to burn down for 45 minutes until a 5cm bed of glowing white coals exists. Arrange three support stones (trivet) around the hottest zone.
buildSteps.warningNote: Maintain ventilation — indoor cooking over open coals produces lethal levels of CO (Carbon Monoxide)

buildSteps.title: Toxin removal (The Leaching Step)
buildSteps.description: For plants like Bitter Cassava or Acorns: grind/shred, then wash in multiple changes of boiling water. This removes water-soluble toxins like cyanide and tannins. Discard the wash water.
buildSteps.warningNote: Raw bitter cassava contains enough cyanide to kill an adult; leaching is a mandatory engineering step, not an option

buildSteps.title: Simmering and pathogen reduction
buildSteps.description: Place vessel on supports. Bring water to a simmer (small bubbles). Add ingredients. Maintain heat for at least 20 minutes for stews. For meat, cook until the center is no longer 'pink' or reaches 74°C.
buildSteps.warningNote: Trichinosis in wild game (pigs, bears) requires thorough cooking to the bone

buildSteps.title: The Earth Oven (Alternative for Large Loads)
buildSteps.description: Dig a pit. Line with stones. Build a large fire on top. Once stones are glowing, rake out wood. Layer green leaves, then food, then more leaves. Bury with 10cm of earth. Wait 4-8 hours. This uses 100% convection/steam.
buildSteps.warningNote: Earth ovens can explode if built on saturated clay; ensure the site is dry

buildSteps.title: Post-cook hygiene
buildSteps.description: Eat immediately while hot. Scour vessels with sand and boiling water. Any leftover food must be reheated to boiling before the next meal to destroy 'storage bacteria' (Bacillus cereus).
buildSteps.warningNote: Grains (rice/maize) left at room temperature develop heat-stable toxins that even re-boiling won't remove

history.event: Anatomical and archaeological arguments propose increased meat processing and possible cooking correlates with smaller teeth and reduced gut size.
Earliest pottery in East Asia suggests a shift to boiling-based diets long before agriculture.
Louis Pasteur formalises the relationship between heat and microbial life, leading to modern food safety standards.

inventors.name: Louis Pasteur
inventors.contribution: Demonstrated heat treatment and microbial causation of spoilage — foundation of pasteurisation science

impact:
Cooking underwrote denser populations and shorter feeding times, freeing hours for tool maintenance and travel. It is prerequisite to many preservation chains (though distinct from fermentation).

Industrial descendants include retorts, extrusion, and precision sous-vide — but the Codex conserves the campfire truth: heat plus container plus knowledge equals transformed biology.

images.caption: Cross-section of an earth oven (Hangi/Umu) showing heat transfer layers
